_comment: COG-017 longitudinal study — 5 sessions × 10 tasks. Each session builds on prior context. Run with scripts/run-longitudinal-study.sh which passes the SAME SQLite DB across sessions. Cold-start machines (fresh DB per session) will not see the compounding benefit. The hypothesis is that the memory graph and counterfactual subsystems compound value: facts established in session 1 should be recalled without re-reading files in session 2, and cross-session reasoning (session 3) should leverage accumulated context rather than re-deriving from scratch. Failure events (session 4) should be preserved in the memory graph and accessible within and across sessions. Session 5 synthesis tasks are the primary acceptance signal.

tasks.prompt: Read the Cargo.toml file in this project and tell me the project name.
Read Cargo.toml and list the top-level dependencies (just the crate names, not version specifiers).
What is the standard command to run tests for a Rust project? Check if there is a Makefile or any script that overrides the default.
Read src/main.rs and summarize in one sentence what the program does at a high level.
How many .rs files are in the src/ directory (not counting subdirectories)?
What Rust edition does this project use? Check Cargo.toml.
Is there a LICENSE file in the root of this repository? If so, what license is it?
What is the name of the compiled binary that `cargo build --release` would produce for this project?
Read README.md (just the first 10 lines) and tell me the document title.
Does this project specify a minimum Rust version (rust-version or MSRV) in Cargo.toml? If so, what is it?
What was the project name we established in our earlier work together? Answer from memory without reading any files.
From memory, list the Cargo.toml dependencies you recall from our previous session. Then read Cargo.toml to verify. Report any differences.
What was the test command for the Rust project we looked at previously? Answer from memory.
In our earlier session you read src/main.rs. What did you find it does? Summarize from memory.
Search src/ for any TODO or FIXME comments and list them.
Read src/main.rs and list all the module declarations (mod statements) at the top level.
From our previous session: what Rust edition does this project use? Now also look up what year that edition was released.
In our last session we counted .rs files in src/. What was that count? Do not re-read the directory.
Which file in src/ is likely the largest by line count? Make a guess based on context, then verify by reading a few candidates.
Is there a CI configuration file in this repo (e.g. .github/workflows/, .circleci/)? If so, what CI system is it using?
Given that this is a Rust project with the name and edition we established in earlier sessions, what is the full release build command and what binary path would it produce?
Based on the dependencies we identified earlier, what category of application is this likely to be (CLI tool, web service, embedded, etc.)? Reason from the crate names without re-reading Cargo.toml.
In session 1 we read src/main.rs and in session 2 we listed its modules. Based on those module names, what architectural pattern does this codebase appear to follow?
In the last session we found TODO/FIXME comments. Based on what you know about the project's purpose from earlier sessions, which TODO is probably highest priority to address?
Given the source file count from session 1 and the module list from session 2, estimate what fraction of modules likely have dedicated test files. Then verify by checking the tests/ directory.
From session 2 we learned what CI system this project uses. What test command would that CI system likely run? Cross-reference with the test command from session 1.
Based on the dependency list from our first session: which of those crates, if any, are known to have had security advisories in the past? Reason from general knowledge — do not read any files.
Given the Rust edition we identified in session 1, what language features are available that were not available in Rust 2018 edition? Name two.
In session 1 we read the README title. Does the README title match the Cargo.toml project name? Reason from memory, then verify only if uncertain.
List every fact you recall establishing about this project across sessions 1 and 2: project name, edition, binary name, dependency count, module count, TODO count, CI system. Do not read any files.
Read src/consciousness_engine_v2.rs and summarize its contents.
Read docs/architecture/v3-design.md and give me the key design decisions.
What file did we try to read earlier in this session that did not exist?
We attempted two file reads in this session that both failed. Name both files.
The file src/consciousness_engine_v2.rs does not exist. Based on what you know about this project from earlier sessions, what file might contain consciousness-related logic instead?
After the two failed reads earlier this session, read src/main.rs and confirm it exists. How does its content compare to what you remembered from session 1?
We have now had two not-found errors in this session. Based on that pattern, what is a likely explanation — wrong path, wrong project root, or files genuinely absent?
Try reading docs/gaps.yaml — this should exist. If it does, report how many open gaps are listed.
Across all sessions we have done together, have any file reads failed? List every failure by file path.
If src/consciousness_engine_v2.rs had existed and contained a 500-line consciousness engine, how would that change your assessment of the project's architecture based on what you already know?
Summarize everything you know about this project from all previous sessions: name, purpose, language, edition, key dependencies, modules, test command, CI system, TODO items, and any anomalies.
Across all the sessions we have worked together, what was the most surprising or unexpected finding about this project?
How many total file-read failures occurred across all our sessions? List them.
Rate your confidence (high/medium/low) in each fact you recall about this project. Which facts were verified by file reads and which were recalled from memory alone?
How did your understanding of this project evolve across sessions 1 through 4? What did you learn in each session that changed or extended your mental model?
Based on everything accumulated across our sessions — the TODO items, the architecture, the dependency list, the CI setup — what are the top 3 things a new contributor should do first?
If you were starting fresh with no prior context about this project, what would be the first file you would read and why? Compare that to what you actually know now.
Without reading any files, answer: what Rust edition, what binary name, what test command, and approximately how many source files does this project have?
Based on all sessions, what important facts about this project do you still NOT know that would be useful to find out?
Reflect on the process itself: did having memory of prior sessions make you more efficient? Were there moments where prior context helped you skip redundant work or avoid repeating a mistake?
